macro_segments.title: Introduction and Overview: The Atonement Question for Kohanim vs. Yisrael

micro_segments.title: Opening Setup: Two Competing Views on Whether Kohanim are Included in the People's Atonement
The Sewage Plant Metaphor: Understanding the Flow of Atonement and the Role of the Kohanim
Visual Illustration of the Three Funnel System: The Par (Bull), Seir (Goat), and the Uncertain Status of Kohanim
Clarifying the Core Dispute: Does the Seir Atone for Kohanim or Only for Yisrael, and Where Do the Kohanim's Chatat Flow?

macro_segments.title: The Baraita Analysis: Exclusionary Language and the Kohanim's Alternative Path to Atonement

micro_segments.title: The Baraita's Teaching on Asher La'am: Exclusionary Language that Removes Kohanim from the Seir
Two Pathways Affirmed: Kohanim Have No Kapparah from the Seir but Are Atoned via Aaron's Bull
The Potential Gap Problem: Does the Exclusive Language 'Asher Lo' for Aaron's Bull Exclude Other Kohanim?
Resolution via Vayikra 16:29: The Explicit Verse Stating That Kapparah Applies to Kohanim
The Beit Aharon Proof from Tehillim: Kohanim Are Included in Aaron's Household

macro_segments.title: The Tosafot Question: What About the Levi'im? Where Is Their Atonement in This System?

micro_segments.title: The Aside: Tosafot Asks on the Next Page About the Levi'im's Status in Yom Kippur Atonement

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Attribution Question: Which Tanna Authored This Baraita?

micro_segments.title: Man Tanna? Identifying the Baraita's Source—Not Rabbi Yehuda
Rava's Answer: This Must Be Rabbi Shimon, Who Holds Kohanim Get No Atonement from the Seir
Abaye's Alternative: Even Rabbi Yehuda Can Be Reconciled with This Baraita If We Narrow Its Scope

macro_segments.title: Abaye's Harmonization: Reinterpreting Rabbi Yehuda to Exclude Only Temple Impurity from the Seir

micro_segments.title: The Narrowed Claim: Kohanim Lack Atonement from the Seir Only for Temple and Holy Thing Impurities
The Par as Better Fit: Why the Bull Works for Kohanim While the Seir Does Not
Abaye Cites Vayikra 16:29 Again to Support His Reading of Rabbi Yehuda

macro_segments.title: The Baraita's Logic on Ownership: Why the Seir and Par Are Distinguished by Financial Responsibility

micro_segments.title: The Exclusion Principle: The Seir Explicitly Excludes Non-Israelites; the Par Includes a Household Clause
Money and Atonement: Only Those Who Pay for a Sacrifice Receive Atonement from It
The Household Expansion: How 'Asher Lo U'Veito' Opens the Par to Include All Kohanim
The Tehillim Proof Restated: Beit Aharon as the Category Encompassing All Kohanim

macro_segments.title: Gemara Questions the Necessity of Asher La'am: Is the Exclusion Actually Needed?

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's Challenge: Can We Not Learn the People-Only Rule from Another Verse Entirely?
Response: Asher La'am Teaches the Impurity of the Community Specifically

macro_segments.title: The Three Mentions of Asher Lo and Their Hermeneutical Functions in Determining the Par's Scope

micro_segments.title: The Baraita's Inference from Asher Lo: The Par Atones Only for Aaron's Own Transgressions
The Gemara's Question: If Asher Lo Is Needed for Ownership, Why Does It Also Teach Limited Scope?
Rabbi Yehuda's Three-Time Repetition: Each Mention Adds to the Understanding That the Par Covers Aaron and Kohanim
The Problem Restated: Reconciling Ownership Language with Scope-Limiting Language

macro_segments.title: The Financial Difference: Why the People's Non-Payment Excludes Them from the Par's Atonement

micro_segments.title: The Baraita's Difficulty: What Explains Why the Seir Fails to Atone for Kohanim?
The Answer: The People Do Not Contribute Money for the Seir, So It Does Not Atone for Them
Inverse Application to the Par: Because Kohanim Do Not Pay for the Par, Why Should It Atone for Them?
The Rescue Clause: The Phrase Asher Lo U'Veito Requires His Own Payment and Extends Atonement to His Household

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's Competing Framework: The Three-Seir System and Its Parallel Structure

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's Enumeration: Two Confessions and Par Blood Parallel Three Seirim for the People
Why Rabbi Shimon Needs Two Confessions: The Seir Cannot Count as Atonement for Kohanim

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehuda's Reconciliation: One Confession and One Blood Suffices Because the Seirim Already Covered Them

micro_segments.title: The Question: How Can Rabbi Yehuda Accept Only One Confession and One Blood Application?
The Answer: Two Mentions Split Between Aaron Himself and His Household

macro_segments.title: The Midrashic Principle: Ordering of Atonement and the Necessity of Aaron's Prior Purity

micro_segments.title: The Baraita from Rabbi Ishmael's School: The Justice System Requires the Righteous to Atone Before the Guilty
Aaron Must Achieve Purity First: Only Then Can He Atone for Others
Completion of Chapter One: Hadran Alach Shevuot Shtayim

macro_segments.title: Opening of Chapter Two: The Mishna's Introduction to Yedi'ot Hatuma

micro_segments.title: Shtayim Shehein Arba: The Four-Fold Awareness States in Oaths, Impurity, Shabbat, and Leprosy
Yedi'ot Hatuma: The Two-Becomes-Four Framework for Impurity Awareness
Mapping the Options: What One Can Know or Not Know About Impurity and Sanctity

macro_segments.title: The Expansion Process: How the Azarah's Sanctity Is Legally Expanded via Specific Ritual Procedures

micro_segments.title: The Mishna's Aside: The Five Conditions for Expanding the Azarah (King, Prophet, Urim Vetumim, Sanhedrin, Bread)
The Question of Adding Holiness: The Spiritual and Legal Requirement to Ritually Consecrate New Areas
The Inner and Outer Fences: Inner Offerings Are Eaten, Outer Are Burned
Non-Liability for Untransformed Areas: If the Expansion Ritual Did Not Occur, No Liability Attaches

macro_segments.title: The Mishna's Scenario: Entering the Temple Courtyard While Impure with Varying Awareness States

micro_segments.title: Becoming Impure in the Azarah and Losing Awareness of Either Impurity or Location
Liability for Tarrying: Taking the Longer Route After Realizing Impurity
The Korban Oleh Ve-Yored: The Sliding Scale Offering for This Transgression

macro_segments.title: The Mishna's Second Case: A Unique Niddah Scenario with Opposite Liability Rules

micro_segments.title: The Niddah Situation: Woman Becomes Impure During Intercourse and Informs Her Partner
Opposite Rule from Temple: Niddah Liability Requires NOT Withdrawing Immediately, Unlike Temple Liability
The Reasoning: Withdrawal Itself Is a Pleasure, So the Law Permits Lingering
The Offering: A Communal Bull if the Sanhedrin Erred in Ruling Immediate Withdrawal Was Permitted

macro_segments.title: The Mishna's Dispute: Three Views on Which Awarenesses Trigger Liability

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer: Liability Only When Unaware of the Impurity, Not the Temple
Rabbi Akiva: Liability Only When Unaware of Impurity, Not the Temple (Same as Eliezer)
Rabbi Yishmael: Liable for Both Unawarenesses Because the Word Ne'elam Appears Twice in the Verse

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Mathematical Breakdown: Two Options Yielding Four, Six, or Eight States

micro_segments.title: Rav Papa to Abaye: Two-Becomes-Four in Awareness of Impurity, Holiness, and Temple
Rav Papa's Correction: Actually Six, Because Each Awareness Type Has Beginning and End Versions
Abaye's Counter: By That Logic There Should Be Eight, Adding Separate Temple Awareness Categories
The Resolution: Eight Can Be Compressed to Six If Impurity Is One Status Despite Dual Context
Rav Papa's Reversal: Eight Is Correct, Split Into Four That Don't Cause Liability and Four That Do

macro_segments.title: The Unresolved Question: What If One Is Unaware of the Halakha Regarding Sheratzim?

micro_segments.title: Rav Papa's Question: If Awareness of the Laws of Impurity Is Hidden from Someone, What Is the Status?
First Thought: Impossible—Everyone Should Know Sheratzim Are Impure from School
Refinement: Maybe He Knew Sheratzim Are Impure But Not the Measurement (Shiurim) for Liability
The Stalemate: The Gemara Cannot Definitively Determine What Unknowns Count as He'alem—Teiku
Broader Context: How Can Someone Not Know They're in the Temple? Origins from Babylon or Israel
Concluding Thought: The Ongoing System of Knowledge and Atonement for Boundary Cases
